{{Short description|How babies develop the ability to think and know things}}
{{Use American English|date=February 2019}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=February 2019}}

'''Infant cognitive development''' is the first stage of human [[cognitive development]], in the youngest children.  The academic field of infant cognitive development studies of how psychological processes involved in thinking and knowing develop in young children.<ref name="Oakly">{{cite book |last=Oakly |first=L. |title=Cognitive Development |publisher=Routledge |year=2004 |location=New York}}</ref> Information is acquired in a number of ways including through sight, sound, touch, taste, smell and language, all of which require processing by our cognitive system.<ref name="Esgate">{{cite book |last1=Esgate |first1=A. |title=Introduction to applied cognitive psychology |last2=Groome |first2=D. |last3=Baker |first3=K. |last4=Heathcote |first4=D. |last5=Kemp |first5=R. |last6=Maguire |first6=M. |last7=Reed |first7=C. |publisher=Psychology Press |year=2005 |location=New York, NY}}</ref>

Scientific investigation in this field has its origin in the first half of the 20th century, an early and influential theory in this field is [[Jean Piaget]]'s [[theory of cognitive development]]. Since Piaget's contribution to the field, infant cognitive development and methods for its investigation have advanced considerably, with numerous psychologists investigating different areas of cognitive development including memory, language and perception, coming up with various theories<ref name="bremner">{{cite book |last=Bremner |first=JG |url=https://archive.org/details/infancy0000brem_s1x4 |title=Infancy |publisher=Blackwell |year=1994 |isbn=978-0-631-18466-9 |edition=2 |url-access=registration}}</ref>—for example [[Neo-Piagetian theories of cognitive development]].

== Overview ==
{{Main|Nature versus nurture}}
[[Tabula rasa]] is an idea (by now a discredited theory) that, at birth, the human mind is a "blank slate" without any rules for processing data, that data is added and rules for processing it are formed solely by one's [[sensory experience]]s. The modern idea of the theory is mostly attributed to [[John Locke]]'s ''[[An Essay Concerning Human Understanding]]'', written in the 17th century.

Its corollary, nativism, argues that we are born with certain [[cognitive modules]] that allow us to learn and acquire certain skills, such as language, (for example the theory of [[Universal Grammar]], the theory that the 'programming' for grammar is hardwired in the brain) and is most associated with the recent work of [[Noam Chomsky]], [[Jerry Fodor]], and [[Steven Pinker]].

If one accepts that nothing is known until learned, and that everyone shares a basic [[common sense]], it appears infants must—to some degree—make some specific [[ontological]] [[inference]]s about how the world works, and what kinds of things it contains. This procedure is studied in psychology and its validity is studied in philosophy.
{{blockquote|We acquire these ordinary [common sense] beliefs at an early age and we take them for granted in everyday life; ... Then, because we are also self-reflective creatures, we turn back on our commonsense assumptions and find them to be more puzzling and problematic than we had bargained for. The concepts we habitually employ raise the kinds of disturbing questions we call philosophical'.|[[Colin McGinn]], Problems in Philosophy. 1993|<ref>{{cite book |last=McGinn|first=Colin|date=1993|title=Problems in Philosophy, The Limits of Inquiry|url=https://archive.org/details/problemsphilosop00mcgi_128|url-access=limited|publisher=Blackwell Publishing|page=[https://archive.org/details/problemsphilosop00mcgi_128/page/n13 8] |isbn=978-1-55786-475-8}}</ref>}}

The notion of [[shared intentionality]], a category of processes during social learning at the onset of life when organisms in the simple-reflexes substage of the sensorimotor stage of cognitive development do not maintain communication via the [[sensory system]],<ref name="Tomasello 2019" /> proposes another approach to the problem: [[Cognition]] is fostered by social bonds between children and caregivers, which gradually increase through [[shared intentionality]].<ref name="Tomasello 2019">Tomasello, M. (2019). "Becoming human: A theory of ontogeny." Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA: [[Harvard University Press]].</ref> Based on recent insights in [[neuroscience]] research, proponents argue that this collaborative interaction emerges in the mother-child pairs at birth for sharing the essential sensory stimulus of the actual cognitive problem.<ref name="Val Danilov 2023 Origin of Perception">{{Cite journal |last=Val Danilov |first=Igor |date=2023 |title=Low-Frequency Oscillations for Nonlocal Neuronal Coupling in Shared Intentionality Before and After Birth: Toward the Origin of Perception |url=https://www.lidsen.com/journals/neurobiology/neurobiology-07-04-192 |journal=OBM Neurobiology |language=en |volume=7 |issue=4 |pages=1–17 |doi=10.21926/obm.neurobiol.2304192 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Val Danilov |first1=Igor |last2=Mihailova |first2=Sandra |date=2022 |title=A New Perspective on Assessing Cognition in Children through Estimating Shared Intentionality |journal=Journal of Intelligence |language=en |volume=10 |issue=2 |page=21 |doi=10.3390/jintelligence10020021 |issn=2079-3200 |pmc=9036231 |pmid=35466234 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

== Jean Piaget ==
Through observations of children, [[Jean Piaget]] established a theory of cognitive development. According to [[Piaget's theory of cognitive development]] there are four stages of cognitive development.<ref name="Piaget">{{cite book |last1=Piaget |first1=J. |url=https://archive.org/details/psychologyofchil00piag |title=The psychology of the child |last2=Inhelder |first2=B. |publisher=Basic Books. |year=1969 |url-access=registration}}</ref><ref name="Piaget2">{{cite book |last=Piaget |first=J. |title=Handbook of child psychology |publisher=Wiley |year=1970 |editor1-last=Mussen |editor1-first=P. |volume=1 |location=New York |chapter=Piaget's theory}}</ref>

# Sensorimotor Stage (Birth to 24 Months)
# Preoperational Stage (24 Months to 7 Years)
# Concrete Operational Stage (7 Years to 12 Years)
# Formal Operational Stage (12 Years and Up)

Infant cognitive development occurs in the Sensorimotor stage which starts at birth and extends until the infant is about 2 years of age. The sensorimotor stage is made up of six sub-stages.
{| class="wikitable"
|+
!Stage
!Age
!What's Happening
!Example
|-
|Stage 1 – Reflexes
|Birth - 6 weeks
|Infants primarily interact with the world through reflexes, such as sucking, grasping, and looking.
|A newborn automatically sucks on anything that touches their lips.
|-
|Stage 2 – Primary Circular Reactions
|6 weeks - 4 months
|Infants begin to repeat actions that bring them pleasure or interesting sensations. These actions are focused on their own body.
|A baby repeatedly sucks their thumb because it feels good.
|-
|Stage 3 – Secondary Circular Reactions
|4 months - 8 months 
|Infants start to combine actions to achieve a goal. They show intentional behavior and can anticipate events.
|A baby repeatedly shakes a rattle to hear the sound it makes.
|-
|Stage 4 – Coordination of Secondary Circular Reactions
|8 months - 12 months
|Infants start to combine actions to achieve a goal. They show intentional behavior and can anticipate events.
|A baby pushes aside a toy to reach another toy underneath it.
|-
|Stage 5 – Tertiary Circular Reactions
|12 months - 18 months
|Infants actively explore their environment and experiment with new behaviors. They try out different actions to see what happens.
|A toddler drops different objects from their high chair to see how they fall.
|-
|Stage 6 – Mental Representation
|18 months - 24 months
|Infants begin to develop mental representations of objects and events. They can think about things that are not physically present and start to engage in pretend play.
|A toddler pretends to feed a doll with an empty spoon.
|}
In the sensorimotor stage, language is absent however infants do show signs of the development of intelligence via action schemes, the basic ways of learning about the physical world.

This begins with reflexes, fixed and predictable behavioral responses. Reflexes which can be practiced develop into action schemes via assimilation, fitting new experiences into what they already know.

The conditioning of reflexes causes the development of habits, systematic coordinated behaviors, but infants fail to understand the causality of their behavior.

They then develop coordination, involving grasping and manipulating objects to know how their actions cause things to occur, but without understanding why it occurs, in a form of practical intelligence. They then divide different objects by their physical properties.

Once action schemes are formed, they can be connected and differentiated using accommodation based on where and when objects exist.

Finally, infants are able to connect and differentiate their internal world with the external world.

== Lev Vygotsky ==
[[Lev Vygotsky]] was also very influential in cognitive development theory. His theory included the [[Zone of proximal development]].<ref name="Vygotsky">{{cite book |last=Vygotsky |first=L. S. |title=Mind in society: The development of higher psychological processes |publisher=Harvard University Press |year=1978 |location=Cambridge, Massachusetts}}</ref> Vygotsky also believed that social and cultural factors contributed heavily to cognitive development.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Harré |first1=Rom |title=Key thinkers in psychology |date=2006 |publisher=Sage Publ. |isbn=978-1-4129-0344-8 |edition=Repr. |location=London [u.a.]}}</ref> Vygotsky argued that development first takes place socially as infants observe their parent's behavior and try to imitate it. As this imitation occurs, parents will guide their children, correcting them and provide challenges for them. Play is an integral part of cognitive development according to Vygotsky, as it is through this play that children gain confidence in their language skills, and start regulating their own thought processes. Through his research Vygotsky suggested that a child's performance differs depending on whether they are solving a problem alone or if another child or adult is assisting them. He refers to this difference as the "zone of proximal development". The theory states that if a child is learning to complete a task, and a more competent person is able to provide assistance, then the child is able to move into a new zone of development and problem solving. Vygotsky refers to this movement through assistance as "scaffolding" and helps bridge the gap between the child's current cognitive abilities and their full potential.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Lev Vygotsky's Cognitive Development Theory and the Benefits of Play |url=http://www.childdevelopmentmedia.com/articles/play-the-work-of-lev-vygotsky/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180227150832/http://www.childdevelopmentmedia.com/articles/play-the-work-of-lev-vygotsky |archive-date=February 27, 2018 |access-date=March 30, 2017}}</ref>

== Erik Erikson ==
[[Erik Erikson]] was a prominent developmental psychologist, who produced a psychoanalytical theory of psychosocial behavior, showing 8 stages of development from infancy to adulthood. At each stage the individual is set with a potential conflict, and either success or failure at each point will go on to determine the outcome of the psychological state of the person. The first stage of development runs from birth to 18 months and thus covers the infancy period. The conflict which Erikson identified during this time was trust vs mistrust. During this vulnerable point in the child's life, they are faced with uncertainties in the world and are therefore reliant on their caregiver. If the child receives consistent care then Erikson claimed that the infant would develop a sense of trust. However, if the care received has been unreliable, mistrust will develop, which may result in heightened feelings of insecurity and anxiety, in future relationships.<ref>Sigelman, Carol. K, Rider, Elizabeth. A, 2012, Life-Span Human Development, Cengage Learning</ref>

== The development of mental processes ==

=== Adaptive nature of cognitive immaturity ===
A hypothesis of pre-perceptual multimodal integration explains an adaptive nature of cognitive development and converges two competing naturalist and constructivist viewpoints about cognition.<ref name="Reflexes Cognition">{{cite journal | last1=Danilov | first1=Igor Val | last2=Mihailova | first2=Sandra | title=Reflexes and Shared Intentionality in the Origins of Emotions Development: A Scoping Review of Studies on Blinking in Infants | journal=OBM Neurobiology | date=2025 | volume=09 | pages=1–21 | doi=10.21926/obm.neurobiol.2501263 | doi-access=free }}</ref> According to the hypothesis, mental processes such as perception, attention, memory, and awareness begin with the association of affective cues with stimuli responsible for triggering the neuronal pathways of simple reflexes.<ref name="Reflexes Cognition" /> This pre-perceptual multimodal integration can succeed owing to neuronal coherence in mother-child dyads beginning from pregnancy.<ref name="Reflexes Cognition" /> According to professor Igor Val Danilov, the natural neurostimulation of the mother's heart on the embryonal nervous system ensures the balanced development of the embryo's nervous system with the necessary cognitive functions.<ref name="Val Danilov Origin Neurostimulation_2024">{{cite journal | last1=Val Danilov | first1=Igor | title=The Origin of Natural Neurostimulation: A Narrative Review of Noninvasive Brain Stimulation Techniques | journal=OBM Neurobiology | date=2024 | volume=08 | issue=4 | pages=1–23 | doi=10.21926/obm.neurobiol.2404260 | doi-access=free }}</ref> Electromagnetic properties of the mother's heart and its interaction with the mother's own and fetal nervous system (physical laws of electromagnetic interference) form neuronal coherence in the mother-fetus bio-system, providing the template beginning from pregnancy.<ref name="Val Danilov Origin Neurostimulation_2024" /> The cognitive-reflex stimuli conjunctions form simple innate neuronal assemblies, shaping the cognitive neuronal patterns in statistical learning that, as empirical evidence showed, are continuously connected with the neuronal pathways of reflexes.<ref name="Reflexes Cognition" />

Development is typically considered to be something progressive: as we age we move from more simple to more complex structures or behaviors. This causes us to interpret early or immature forms of cognition as incomplete forms of the adult model. This does not always hold true. Immature forms of development can serve some function of their own, as it adapts for the current environment of the infant. For example, infant's relatively poor perceptual skills protect their nervous system from undergoing sensory overload. The fact that infants have slow information processing prevents them from establishing intellectual habits early in their lives that would cause problems later in life, as their environments are significantly different. From this it could be argued that infants and young children's cognitive and perceptual abilities might be designed to be suited to their needs at that particular time in their lives rather than incomplete versions of the more sophisticated models possessed in adults. Hanus Papousek (1977) looked at the concept that learning at an early stage of development may not be beneficial to the infant if it creates overstimulation. In an experiment he conditioned infants to turn their heads to the sound of a buzzer. The training for the task began either at birth or at 31 or at 44 days. He discovered that infants took many more trials and days to learn the task if they learned from birth than the infants who learned later. Infants need stimulation, but if stimulation is too great, it could distract infants and young children from other tasks, and replace other, more crucial activities to their development such as social interaction.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Bjorklund |first1=F. |title=Children's Thinking Cognitive Development and Individual Differences |date=2012 |publisher=SAGE Publications |isbn=9781544361338 |edition=7th}}</ref>

=== Attention ===
{{Main|Attention}}
According to Professor [[Wilhelm Wundt]] (1832-1920), physiologist and philosopher, attention is the concentration of awareness on some phenomenon during a period, which is necessary to elevate the clear [[perception]] of the narrow region of the content of [[consciousness]] and which is feasible to control this focus in [[mind]].<ref name="Wundt_1912">Wilhelm Wundt. (1912). ''Introduction to Psychology,'' trans. Rudolf Pintner (London: Allen, 1912; reprint ed., New York: [[Arno Press]], 1973)</ref> Therefore, attention is a process of controlling thought that continues over time.<ref>Leahey, T.H. (1979). "Something old, something new: Attention in Wundt and modern cognitive psychology." ''Journal of the History of the Behavioral Sciences,'' 15(3), 242-252.</ref> His experiments showed that the size of the focal area of consciousness in adults ranges from three to six items.<ref name="Wundt_1912" /> According to associate professor at [[Riga Stradiņš University|Rīga Stradiņš University]] Sandra Mihailova and research professor in [[bioengineering]] at [https://www.liepu.lv/en Liepaja University] Igor Val Danilov, the development of the scope of attention in young children becomes evident within the first three years of life as they show considerable advances in increasing focal area.<ref name="Val_2022">{{cite journal | last1=Val Danilov | first1=Igor | last2=Mihailova | first2=Sandra | title=A Case Study on the Development of Math Competence in an Eight-year-old Child with Dyscalculia: Shared Intentionality in Human-Computer Interaction for Online Treatment Via Subitizing | journal=OBM Neurobiology | date=2022 | volume=06 | issue=2 | pages=1–17 | doi=10.21926/obm.neurobiol.2202122 | doi-access=free }}</ref> The empirical data has four stages:

* The first stage, with up to two items in the focal point, lasts up to about six months of age.<ref name="Val_2022" /><ref>Starkey, P.; Cooper, R.G.Jr. (1980). "Perception of numbers by human infants." ''Science.''; 210: 1033-1035.</ref>
* The second stage, with up to three items, begins after eight months.<ref name="Val_2022" /><ref name="Starkey_Cooper_1995">Starkey, P.; Cooper, R.G. (1995). "The development of subitizing in young children." ''Br J Dev Psychol.''; 13: 399-420.</ref>
* The third stage appears at about 3.5 years of age with four items.<ref name="Val_2022" /><ref>Gray, S.A.; Reeve, R.A. (2014). "Preschoolers' dot enumeration abilities are markers of their arithmetic competence." ''PLoS ONE.''; 9: e94428.</ref>
* The fourth stage starts in children when they are about five years old and can hold five or more items in the focal point.<ref name="Val_2022" /><ref name="Starkey_Cooper_1995" />

=== Memory ===
{{Main|Memory development}}
The [[Memory development|development of memory in children]] becomes evident within the first 2 to 3 years of a child's life as they show considerable advances in [[declarative memory]]. This enhancement continues into adolescence with major developments in [[short term memory]], [[working memory]], [[long-term memory]] and [[autobiographical memory]].<ref name="bad">{{cite book |last=Siegler |first=R. S. |author-link=Robert S. Siegler |title=Children's Thinking |publisher=Prentice Hall |year=1998 |isbn=978-0-13-397910-7 |edition=3rd |location=Upper Saddle River, NJ}}</ref>

Research on the development of memory has indicated that declarative, or explicit memory, may exist in infants who are even younger than two years old. For example, newborns who are less than 3 days old demonstrate a preference for their mother's own voice.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=DeCasper |first1=A.J. |last2=Fifer |first2=W.P. |year=1980 |title=Of human bonding: Newborns prefer their mothers' voices |journal=Science |volume=208 |issue=4448 |pages=1174–1176 |bibcode=1980Sci...208.1174D |citeseerx=10.1.1.553.1738 |doi=10.1126/science.7375928 |pmid=7375928}}</ref>

=== Perception ===

==== Attribution of causality ====
The perception of causality was initially studied by professor Albert Michotte where he presented adults with animated images of moving balls.<ref name="Michotte">{{cite book |last=Michotte |first=A. |title=The Perception of Causality |publisher=Basic Books |year=1967 |location=New York, NY}}</ref> By manipulating the direction and timing of the moving balls (spatial and temporal dimensions) he was able to influence participants' perception of causality. There is contradicting evidence on whether causal perception is innate and present at birth or whether it is a result of perception development. Through research with very young infants, many studies have shown support for the theory that humans are born with the mechanisms needed for the perception of causality.<ref name="Leslie1">{{cite journal |last=Leslie |first=A. M. |year=1982 |title=The perception of causality in infants |journal=Perception |volume=11 |issue=2 |pages=173–186 |doi=10.1068/p110173 |pmid=7155770 |s2cid=2901777}}</ref><ref name="Leslie2">{{cite journal |last=Leslie |first=A. M. |year=1984 |title=Spatiotemporal continuity and the perception of causality in infants |journal=Perception |volume=13 |issue=3 |pages=287–305 |doi=10.1068/p130287 |pmid=6514514 |s2cid=21381509}}</ref><ref name="SaxeANDCarey">{{cite journal |last1=Saxe |first1=R. |last2=Carey |first2=S. |year=2006 |title=The perception of causality in infancy |journal=Acta Psychologica |volume=123 |issue=1–2 |pages=144–165 |doi=10.1016/j.actpsy.2006.05.005 |pmid=16905110 |hdl-access=free |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0012-8F4E-B}}</ref> Recent research has even shown this ability in newborns only a few hours old.<ref name="Mascalzoni">{{cite journal |last1=Mascalzoni |first1=E. |last2=Regolin |first2=L. |last3=Vallortigara |first3=G. |author-link3=Giorgio Vallortigara |last4=Simion |first4=F. |year=2013 |title=The cradle of causal reasoning: newborns' preference for physical causality |journal=Developmental Science |volume=16 |issue=3 |pages=327–335 |doi=10.1111/desc.12018 |pmid=23587033}}</ref> However, other studies have shown similar results received by Michotte (1976) in infants as young as 6 months, but not younger.<ref name="Cohen">{{cite journal |last1=Cohen |first1=L. B. |last2=Amsel |first2=G. |year=1998 |title=Precursors to infants' perception of the causality of a simple event |journal=Infant Behavior & Development |volume=21 |issue=4 |pages=713–731 |doi=10.1016/s0163-6383(98)90040-6}}</ref><ref name="Oakes">{{cite journal |last=Oakes |first=L. M. |year=1994 |title=Development of infants' use of continuity cues in their perception of causality |journal=Developmental Psychology |volume=30 |issue=6 |pages=869–879 |doi=10.1037/0012-1649.30.6.869}}</ref> These studies support a more developmental progression of abilities required for the perception of causality.

==== Object permanence ====
[[Object permanence]] is the understanding that an object continues to exist, even when one cannot see it or touch it. It is an important milestone in the stages of cognitive development for infants. Numerous tests regarding it have been done, usually involving a toy and a crude barrier which is placed in front of the toy, and then removed repeatedly ([[peekaboo]]). In early [[sensorimotor stage]]s, the infant is completely unable to comprehend object permanence. Psychologist [[Jean Piaget]] conducted experiments with infants which led him to conclude that this awareness was typically achieved at eight to nine months of age. Infants before this age are too young to understand object permanence, which explains why infants at this age do not cry when their mothers are gone – "Out of sight, out of mind". A lack of object permanence can lead to [[A-not-B error]]s, where children look for an object at the location where they first discovered it rather than where they have just seen it placed.

==== Depth perception ====
Studies in psychology<ref>{{Cite web |title=The 'Visual Cliff' |url=http://www.wadsworth.com/psychology_d/templates/student_resources/0155060678_rathus/ps/ps05.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190406153925/http://www.wadsworth.com/psychology_d/templates/student_resources/0155060678_rathus/ps/ps05.html |archive-date=April 6, 2019 |access-date=September 26, 2016 |website=Library for Psychology in the New Millennium}}</ref> also suggest that [[three dimensionality]] and [[depth perception]] is not necessarily fully [[Intuition (knowledge)|intuitive]], and must be partially learned in infancy using an [[Visual perception#Unconscious inference|unconscious inference]]. The acquisition of depth perception and its development in infant cognitive systems was researched by professor Richard D. Walk. Walk found that human infants can discriminate depth well from an "innate learned" point of view: they are able to discriminate depth from the age at which they can be tested. However, their visual mechanisms are still maturing. Walk discovered that infants are better able to discriminate depth when there is a definitive pattern separating the deeper and shallower areas than if either one is at all indefinite, and that the depth and distance must be of a certain level of distance in order to be successfully distinguished by the infant. According to Walk there is a clear development of perceptual behaviour, as with increasing age it is shown that children are able to discriminate between depths more accurately, and gauge more subtle differences between depths.<ref>Walk, Richard D., 1966, The Development of Depth Perception in Animals and Human Infants, Wiley</ref>

==== Physical laws ====
Largely thanks to the innovative strategies developed by professor [[Renee Baillargeon]] and her colleagues, significant insights have been developed regarding how young infants comprehend natural physical laws. Much of this research depends on carefully observing when infants react as if events are unexpected. For example, if an infant sees an object that appears to be suspended in mid-air, and behaves as if this is unexpected, then this suggests that the infant has an understanding that things usually fall if they are not supported. Baillargeon and her colleagues have contributed evidence, for example, about infants' understanding of object permanence<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Baillargeon |first1=R. |last2=DeVos |first2=J. |year=1991 |title=Object permanence in young infants: Further evidence |journal=Child Development |volume=62 |issue=6 |pages=1227–1246 |doi=10.2307/1130803 |jstor=1130803 |pmid=1786712}}</ref> and their reasoning about hidden objects.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Baillargeon |first1=R |year=2004 |title=Infants' reasoning about hidden objects: Evidence for event-general and event-specific expectations |journal=Developmental Science |volume=7 |issue=4 |pages=391–424 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-7687.2004.00357.x |pmc=4215973 |pmid=15484586}}</ref>

==== Shared intentionality ====
The noted above hypotheses plausibly explain [[perception]] development when the nervous system of the young organism has already mastered the mode to distinguish relevant stimuli from the cacophony of electromagnetic waves, chemical interactions, and pressure fluctuations. The [[Shared intentionality]] approach attempts to describe environmental learning in the previous developmental period (even before birth)<ref>{{cite book | last1=Val Danilov | first1=Igor | title=Information Systems and Technological Advances for Sustainable Development | chapter=Child Cognitive Development with the Maternal Heartbeat: A Mother-Fetus Neurocognitive Model and Architecture for Bioengineering Systems | series=Lecture Notes in Information Systems and Organisation | date=2024 | volume=71 | pages=216–223 | doi=10.1007/978-3-031-75329-9_24 | isbn=978-3-031-75328-2 }}</ref> when nothing is still known, and the young organism just learns how to assimilate basic common meanings.<ref name="Val Danilov 2023 Origin of Perception" /> It still needs to overcome the [[binding problem]] to choose relevant stimuli for primary data entry.  Neuroscience research studies <ref>{{cite journal | last1=Liu | first1=Jieqiong | last2=Zhang | first2=Ruqian | last3=Xie | first3=Enhui | last4=Lin | first4=Yixuan | last5=Chen | first5=Danni | last6=Liu | first6=Yang | last7=Li | first7=Keshuang | last8=Chen | first8=Mei | last9=Li | first9=Yangzhuo | last10=Wang | first10=Guanghai | last11=Li | first11=Xianchun | title=Shared intentionality modulates interpersonal neural synchronization at the establishment of communication system | journal=Communications Biology | date=2023 | volume=6 | issue=1 | article-number=832 | doi=10.1038/s42003-023-05197-z | pmid=37563301 | pmc=10415255 }}</ref><ref>Painter, D.R., Kim, J.J., Renton, A.I., Mattingley, J.B. (2021). "Joint control of visually guided actions involves concordant increases in behavioural and neural coupling." ''Commun Biol.'' 2021; 4: 816.</ref><ref>Hu, Y., Pan, Y., Shi, X., Cai, Q., Li, X., Cheng, X. (2018). "Inter-brain synchrony and cooperation context in interactive decision making." ''Biol Psychol.'' 2018; 133: 54-62.</ref><ref>Fishburn, F.A., Murty, V.P., Hlutkowsky, C.O., MacGillivray, C.E., Bemis, L.M., Murphy, M.E., et al. (2018). "Putting our heads together: Interpersonal neural synchronization as a biological mechanism for shared intentionality." ''Soc Cogn Affect Neurosci.'' 2018; 13: 841-849.</ref><ref>Szymanski, C., Pesquita, A., Brennan, A.A., Perdikis, D., Enns, J.T., Brick, T.R., et al. (2017). "Teams on the same wavelength perform better: Inter-brain phase synchronization constitutes a neural substrate for social facilitation." ''Neuroimage.'' 2017; 152: 425-436.</ref><ref>Astolfi, L., Toppi, J., De Vico Fallani, F., Vecchiato, G., Salinari, S., Mattia, D., et al. (2010). "Neuroelectrical hyperscanning measures simultaneous brain activity in humans." ''Brain Topogr.'' 2010; 23: 243-256.</ref> declared observed inter-brain activity under conditions without communication in pairs while subjects were solving the shared cognitive problem, and they registered an increased inter-brain activity in contrast to the condition when subjects solved a similar problem alone. These data show that collaborative interaction without sensory cues can emerge in mother-child dyads, providing Shared intentionality, which indicates to the immature organism the essential sensory stimulus of the actual cognitive problem.<ref name="Val Danilov 2023 Origin of Perception" /> Latvian professor Igor Val Danilov proposed the [[Shared intentionality]] hypothesis, which attempts to explain neurophysiological processes at the beginning of cognitive development at different levels of interaction, from interpersonal dynamics to neuronal interactions.<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Danilov | first1=Igor Val | title=Theoretical Grounds of Shared Intentionality for Neuroscience in Developing Bioengineering Systems | journal=OBM Neurobiology | date=2023 | volume=07 | pages=1–14 | doi=10.21926/obm.neurobiol.2301156 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Val Danilov |first=Igor |date=2023 |title=Shared Intentionality Modulation at the Cell Level: Low-Frequency Oscillations for Temporal Coordination in Bioengineering Systems |url=https://www.lidsen.com/journals/neurobiology/neurobiology-07-04-185 |journal=OBM Neurobiology |language=en |volume=7 |issue=4 |pages=1–17 |doi=10.21926/obm.neurobiol.2304185 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

=== Language ===
{{Main|Language acquisition}}
From birth, babies are learning to communicate. The communication begins with crying and then begins to develop into cooing and babbling. Infants develop their speech by mimicking those around them. Gestures and facial expressions are all part of language development. In the first three months of life babies will generally use different crying types to express their different needs, as well as making other sounds such as cooing. They will begin mimicking facial expressions and smiling at the sight of familiar faces. Between the ages of 4–6 months infants have a greater response towards different tones in voices, and greater engagement, watching the speaker's face. The child's own language skills develop with larger variation in babbling sounds, and elicit responses in conversation through babbling. From 7 months to the end of their first year babies are able to understand frequently heard words and can respond to simple requests. Their babbling becomes more complex and they communicate with it as if they are making sense, they use babbling to express their desires. Non-verbal communication also develops and actions such as waving goodbye are produced. This is also the period in which babies often say their first word, an important milestone in the child's life.<ref>Kim, Yaebin, Byington, Teresea, 2015, Infant Language Development, University of Nevada Cooperative Extension</ref>

There are a number of theories as to how children acquire language, including the work of Skinner, who argued that language is learnt via reinforcement.  Skinner argued that echoic verbal behavior is essential to child language acquisition. Parents reinforce language by responding with attention and correcting mistakes, promoting more accurate language development.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Skinner |first=B. F. |title=Verbal behavior |date=1957 |publisher=Prentice-Hall |isbn=978-0-13-941591-3 |series=The century psychology series |location=Englewood Cliffs, NJ}}</ref>

Others argue that language acquisition is innate, such as [[Noam Chomsky|Chomsky]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last1=Chomsky |first1=Noam |title=On nature and language: with an essay on "The secular priesthood and the perils of democracy" |last2=Belletti |first2=Adriana |last3=Rizzi |first3=Luigi |date=2008 |publisher=Cambridge Univ. Press |isbn=978-0-521-01624-7 |edition=Repr |location=Cambridge}}</ref> He argues that all babies are born with an innate language faculty helping them learn language naturally during infancy, later referred to as the LAD (Language Acquisition Device), acquiring language rapidly, without conscious access.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Chomsky |first=Noam |title=Aspects of the theory of syntax |date=2015 |publisher=The MIT Press |isbn=978-0-262-52740-8 |edition=50th Anniversary |series=Massachusetts Institute of Technology |location=Cambridge (Mass.)}}</ref>  This provides them with an inherent understanding of linguistic principles which allows them to learn and produce language, even with limited input from caregivers or the ability to hear speech. This allows humans to produce and understand an infinite number of novel sentences from a finite number of grammatical rules.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Chomsky |first=Noam |title=Language and mind |date=2007 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-0-521-67493-5 |edition=3. ed. Reprinted |location=Cambridge}}</ref> He acknowledges that the environment acts as a trigger for pre-existing structures for language learning.<ref name=":0" />

Bruner supplements this idea with the LASS (Language Acquisition Support System)<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Bruner |first1=Jerome S. |title=Child's talk: learning to use language |last2=Watson |first2=Rita |date=1983 |publisher=W.W. Norton |isbn=978-0-393-01753-3 |edition=1st |location=New York}}</ref> involving the support and 'scaffolding' by caregivers. This involves correction of poor language, expanding vocabulary, modelling sentences, and prompting of speech.

=== Empathy ===
[[Empathy]] involves the ability to recognize one's own and others feelings, and regulate emotional response accordingly.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Empathy and its development |date=1990 |publisher=Cambridge Univ. Press |isbn=978-0-521-40986-5 |editor-last=Eisenberg |editor-first=Nancy |edition=1. paperback |series=Cambridge studies in social and emotional development |location=Cambridge |editor-last2=Strayer |editor-first2=Janet}}</ref> In the first few days of life, babies are able to discriminate distress cues by human voices<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cheng |first1=Yawei |last2=Lee |first2=Shin-Yi |last3=Chen |first3=Hsin-Yu |last4=Wang |first4=Ping-Yao |last5=Decety |first5=Jean |date=2012-06-01 |title=Voice and Emotion Processing in the Human Neonatal Brain |url=https://direct.mit.edu/jocn/article/24/6/1411/27783/Voice-and-Emotion-Processing-in-the-Human-Neonatal |journal=Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience |language=en |volume=24 |issue=6 |pages=1411–1419 |doi=10.1162/jocn_a_00214 |pmid=22360593 |issn=0898-929X|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and cry when other babies cry.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Geangu |first1=Elena |last2=Benga |first2=Oana |last3=Stahl |first3=Daniel |last4=Striano |first4=Tricia |date=2011 |title=Individual Differences in Infants' Emotional Resonance to a Peer in Distress: Self–Other Awareness and Emotion Regulation |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9507.2010.00596.x |journal=Social Development |language=en |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=450–470 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9507.2010.00596.x |issn=1467-9507}}</ref> From 3–7 months, infants can identify sad vocalizations and facial expressions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Blasi |first1=Anna |last2=Mercure |first2=Evelyne |last3=Lloyd-Fox |first3=Sarah |last4=Thomson |first4=Alex |last5=Brammer |first5=Michael |last6=Sauter |first6=Disa |last7=Deeley |first7=Quinton |last8=Barker |first8=Gareth J. |last9=Renvall |first9=Ville |last10=Deoni |first10=Sean |last11=Gasston |first11=David |last12=Williams |first12=Steven C.R. |last13=Johnson |first13=Mark H. |last14=Simmons |first14=Andrew |last15=Murphy |first15=Declan G.M. |date=2011 |title=Early Specialization for Voice and Emotion Processing in the Infant Brain |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0960982211006543 |journal=Current Biology |language=en |volume=21 |issue=14 |pages=1220–1224 |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2011.06.009|pmid=21723130 |bibcode=2011CBio...21.1220B |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0012-0652-C |hdl-access=free }}</ref> From 5–10 months, they show a preference for prosocial characters in shows, being aversive to harm caused by others.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hamlin |first1=J. Kiley |last2=Wynn |first2=Karen |last3=Bloom |first3=Paul |date=2007 |title=Social evaluation by preverbal infants |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/nature06288 |journal=Nature |language=en |volume=450 |issue=7169 |pages=557–559 |doi=10.1038/nature06288 |pmid=18033298 |bibcode=2007Natur.450..557H |issn=1476-4687|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Children at 24 months are aware of their wrongdoing,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dahl |first1=Audun |last2=Brownell |first2=Celia A. |date=2019-06-01 |title=The Social Origins of Human Prosociality |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |language=EN |volume=28 |issue=3 |pages=274–279 |doi=10.1177/0963721419830386 |issn=0963-7214 |pmc=6786781 |pmid=31602098}}</ref> and show shame-like responses from 29 months. Infants with a secure attachment, related to consistent parental care, appear to show more empathy.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Panfile |first1=Tia M. |last2=Laible |first2=Deborah J. |date=2012 |title=Attachment Security and Child's Empathy: The Mediating Role of Emotion Regulation |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/article/461521 |journal=Merrill-Palmer Quarterly |volume=58 |issue=1 |pages=1–21 |doi=10.1353/mpq.2012.0003 |issn=1535-0266|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This suggests that empathy is not an automatically developed skill, but one that is activated based on experience.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Decety |first1=Jean |last2=Holvoet |first2=Claire |date=2021-12-01 |title=The emergence of empathy: A developmental neuroscience perspective |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S027322972100054X |journal=Developmental Review |volume=62 |article-number=100999 |doi=10.1016/j.dr.2021.100999 |issn=0273-2297|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Metacognition ===
{{Main|Metacognition}}

==== Self-awareness ====
The most common technique used in research for testing self-awareness in infants is a mirror test known as the "[[Rouge Test]]".<ref name="Amsterdam">{{cite journal |last=Amsterdam |first=B. |year=1972 |title=Mirror self-image reactions before age two |journal=Developmental Psychobiology |volume=5 |issue=4 |pages=297–305 |doi=10.1002/dev.420050403 |pmid=4679817}}</ref><ref name="Gallup">{{cite journal |last=Gallup |first=G. G. |year=1970 |title=Chimpanzees: Self-recognition |journal=Science |volume=167 |issue=3914 |pages=86–87 |bibcode=1970Sci...167...86G |doi=10.1126/science.167.3914.86 |pmid=4982211 |s2cid=145295899}}</ref> The rouge test works by applying a dot on an infant's face and then placing them in front of the mirror. If the infant investigates the dot on their nose by touching it, they are thought to realize their own existence and have achieved self-awareness. A number of research studies have used this technique and shown self-awareness to develop between 15 and 24 months of age.<ref name="Lewis">{{cite book |last1=Lewis |first1=M. |url=https://archive.org/details/socialcognitiona0000lewi |title=Social cognition and the acquisition of self |last2=Brooks-Gunn |first2=J. |publisher=Plenum Press. |year=1979 |location=New York, NY |isbn=978-0-306-40232-6 |url-access=registration}}</ref><ref name="Rochat2">{{cite journal |last1=Rochat |first1=P. |last2=Broesch |first2=T. |last3=Jayne |first3=K. |year=2012 |title=Social awareness and early self-recognition |journal=Consciousness and Cognition |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=1491–1497 |doi=10.1016/j.concog.2012.04.007 |pmid=22673374 |s2cid=16697739}}</ref> Some researchers take language such as "I, me, my, etc." as an indicator of self-awareness.<ref name="Bates">{{cite book |last=Bates |first=E. |title=The self in transition: Infancy to childhood |publisher=University of Chicago Press |year=1990 |editor1-last=Cicchetti |editor1-first=D. |location=Chicago |pages=165–182 |chapter=Language about me and you: Pronominal reference and the emerging concept of self |editor2-last=Beeghly |editor2-first=M.}}</ref>

Rochat (2003) described a more in-depth developmental path in acquiring self-awareness through various stages.<ref name="Rochat1">{{cite journal |last=Rochat |first=P. |year=2003 |title=Five levels of self-awareness as they unfold early in life |journal=Consciousness and Cognition |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=717–731 |doi=10.1016/s1053-8100(03)00081-3 |pmid=14656513 |s2cid=10241157}}</ref><ref name="Legrain">{{cite journal |last1=Legrain |first1=L. |last2=Cleeremans |first2=A. |last3=Destrebecqz |first3=A. |year=2011 |title=Distinguishing three levels in explicit self-awareness |url=https://dipot.ulb.ac.be/dspace/bitstream/2013/70020/1/3%20levels%20of%20self%20awareness.pdf |journal=Consciousness and Cognition |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=578–585 |doi=10.1016/j.concog.2010.10.010 |pmid=21093301 |hdl=2013/ULB-DIPOT:oai:dipot.ulb.ac.be:2013/70020 |s2cid=18739652}}</ref> He described self-awareness as occurring in 5 stages beginning from birth.
{| class="wikitable"
!Stage
!Description
|-
|Stage 1 – Differentiation
(from birth)
|Right from birth infants are able to differentiate the self from the non-self. A study using the infant rooting reflex found that infants rooted significantly less from self-stimulation, contrary to when the stimulation came from the experimenter.<ref name="Rochat4">{{cite journal |last1=Rochat |first1=P. |last2=Hespos |first2=S. J. |year=1997 |title=Differential rooting response by neonates: evidence for an early sense of self |journal=Early Development and Parenting |volume=6 |issue=2 |pages=150.1–150.8 |doi=10.1002/(sici)1099-0917(199709/12)6:3/4<105::aid-edp150>3.0.co;2-u}}</ref>
|-
|Stage 2 – Situation
(by 2 months)
|In addition to differentiation, infants at this stage can also situate themselves in relation to a model. In one experiment infants were able to imitate tongue orientation from an adult model.<ref name="Meltzoff">{{cite journal |last1=Meltzoff |first1=A. N. |last2=Moore |first2=M. K. |year=1992 |title=Early imitation within a functional framework: The importance of person identity, movement, and development |journal=Infant Behavior and Development |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=479–505 |doi=10.1016/0163-6383(92)80015-m |pmc=4137790 |pmid=25147415}}</ref> Additionally, another sign of the differentiation is when infants bring themselves into contact with objects by reaching for them.<ref name="Rochat3">{{cite journal |last1=Rochat |first1=P. |last2=Goubet |first2=N. |year=1995 |title=Development of sitting and reaching in 5- to 6-month-old infants |journal=Infant Behavior and Development |volume=18 |pages=53–68 |doi=10.1016/0163-6383(95)90007-1}}</ref>
|-
|Stage 3 – Identification
(by 2 years)
|At this stage the more common definition of "self-awareness" comes into play, where infants can identify themselves in a mirror through the "rouge test" as well as begin to use language to refer to themselves.
|-
|Stage 4 – Permanence
|This stage occurs after infancy when children are aware that their sense of self continues to exist across both time and space.
|-
|Stage 5 – Self-consciousness or meta-self-awareness
|This also occurs after infancy. This is the final stage when children can see themselves in 3rd person, or how they are perceived by others.
|}

==== Symbolic thought ====
Symbolic thought refers to the ability to use words, images, and other symbols to represent words or feelings. During the preoperational stage a child's capacity for symbolism increases, this is shown by their increase in language use during this stage. This can also be seen by the way children play with objects, a stick becomes a sword and a box becomes armor. Children in this stage still might not understand that a map represents a real place, and that a picture of food does not have a smell.<ref>Hockenbury, Don and Hockenbury, Sandra "Discovering Psychology:Fifth Edition". Worth Publishers, 2010, p. 389.</ref>

==== Theory of mind ====
[[Theory of mind]] refers to the ability to understand and predict what others are thinking and feeling,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Premack |first1=David |last2=Woodruff |first2=Guy |date=1978 |title=Does the chimpanzee have a theory of mind? |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/identifier/S0140525X00076512/type/journal_article |journal=Behavioral and Brain Sciences |language=en |volume=1 |issue=4 |pages=515–526 |doi=10.1017/S0140525X00076512 |issn=0140-525X}}</ref> involving recognizing that others have different beliefs.  First order theory of mind refers to one's ability to understand their own mental life, while second order theory of mind refers to one's ability to understand the mental lives of others.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Rakoczy |first=Hannes |date=2012 |title=Do infants have a theory of mind? |url=https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.2044-835X.2011.02061.x |journal=British Journal of Developmental Psychology |language=en |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=59–74 |doi=10.1111/j.2044-835X.2011.02061.x |pmid=22429033 |issn=2044-835X|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Infants show early signs of understanding belief-related situations.<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Träuble |first1=Birgit |last2=Marinović |first2=Vesna |last3=Pauen |first3=Sabina |date=2010 |title=Early Theory of Mind Competencies: Do Infants Understand Others' Beliefs? |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1532-7078.2009.00025.x |journal=Infancy |language=en |volume=15 |issue=4 |pages=434–444 |doi=10.1111/j.1532-7078.2009.00025.x |pmid=32693521 |issn=1532-7078|url-access=subscription }}</ref> However, they are generally thought to possess implicit mentalization<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Apperly |first1=Ian A |last2=Butterfill |first2=Stephen A |date=2009 |title=Do humans have two systems to track beliefs and belief-like states |url=https://doi.org/10.1037/a0016923 |journal=Psychological Review |volume=116 |issue=4 |pages=953–970 |doi=10.1037/a0016923 |issn=1939-1471 |pmid=19839692}}</ref>—fast, non-conscious, and non-verbal—rather than explicit mentalization, which is slower, conscious, verbal, and typically emerges around 4 years of age<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wimmer |first1=Heinz |last2=Perner |first2=Josef |date=1983 |title=Beliefs about beliefs: Representation and constraining function of wrong beliefs in young children's understanding of deception |url=https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/0010-0277%2883%2990004-5 |journal=Cognition |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=103–128 |doi=10.1016/0010-0277(83)90004-5 |pmid=6681741 |issn=0010-0277}}</ref>. Implicit mentalization includes, for example, the ability to recognize when a communicative partner has incorrect or incomplete information about a relevant aspect of the world. Multiple independent studies suggest that, in such situations, infants communicate more frequently and accurately to share their knowledge with others<ref>{{Cite journal |last=O'Neill |first=Daniela K. |date=1996 |title=Two-Year-Old Children's Sensitivity to a Parent's Knowledge State When Making Requests |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8624.1996.tb01758.x |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=659–677 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8624.1996.tb01758.x |issn=0009-3920}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Knudsen |first1=Birgit |last2=Liszkowski |first2=Ulf |date=2012 |title=Eighteen- and 24-month-old infants correct others in anticipation of action mistakes |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-7687.2011.01098.x |journal=Developmental Science |language=en |volume=15 |issue=1 |pages=113–122 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-7687.2011.01098.x |pmid=22251297 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0013-408D-6 |issn=1363-755X}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tauzin |first1=Tibor |last2=Call |first2=Josep |last3=Gergely |first3=György |date=2024 |title=Infants Produce Optimally Informative Points to Satisfy the Epistemic Needs of Their Communicative Partner |journal=Open Mind |language=en |volume=8 |pages=1228–1246 |doi=10.1162/opmi_a_00166 |issn=2470-2986 |pmc=11520476 |pmid=39474157}}</ref>. However, infants do not consistently perform well on tasks that require them to predict others’ behavior based on false beliefs, as reflected in the difficulty of replicating some of these findings.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wellman |first1=Henry M. |last2=Cross |first2=David |last3=Watson |first3=Julanne |date=2001 |title=Meta-Analysis of Theory-of-Mind Development: The Truth about False Belief |url=https://srcd.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/1467-8624.00304 |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=72 |issue=3 |pages=655–684 |doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00304 |pmid=11405571 |issn=0009-3920|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Children with [[Autism]] struggle with theory of mind,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Baron-Cohen |first1=Simon |last2=Leslie |first2=Alan M. |last3=Frith |first3=Uta |date=1985-10-01 |title=Does the autistic child have a "theory of mind" ? |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/0010027785900228 |journal=Cognition |volume=21 |issue=1 |pages=37–46 |doi=10.1016/0010-0277(85)90022-8 |pmid=2934210 |issn=0010-0277|url-access=subscription }}</ref> showing mindblindness,<ref>{{Cite book |last=Baron-Cohen |first=Simon |url=https://direct.mit.edu/books/monograph/3890/MindblindnessAn-Essay-on-Autism-and-Theory-of-Mind |title=Mindblindness: An Essay on Autism and Theory of Mind |date=1995-02-24 |publisher=The MIT Press |isbn=978-0-262-26773-1 |language=en |doi=10.7551/mitpress/4635.001.0001}}</ref> a difficulty understanding other people's thoughts and feelings, finding other individuals confusing and unpredictable. This is seen where autistic children show less pretend play<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Baron-Cohen |first=Simon |date=1987 |title=Autism and symbolic play |url=https://bpspsychub.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.2044-835X.1987.tb01049.x |journal=British Journal of Developmental Psychology |language=en |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=139–148 |doi=10.1111/j.2044-835X.1987.tb01049.x |issn=2044-835X|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and lower joint attention.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Swettenham |first1=J. |last2=Baron-Cohen |first2=S. |last3=Charman |first3=T. |last4=Cox |first4=A. |last5=Baird |first5=G. |last6=Drew |first6=A. |last7=Rees |first7=L. |last8=Wheelwright |first8=S. |date=1998 |title=The Frequency and Distribution of Spontaneous Attention Shifts between Social and Nonsocial Stimuli in Autistic, Typically Developing, and Nonautistic Developmentally Delayed Infants |url=http://doi.wiley.com/10.1017/S0021963098002595 |journal=Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry |volume=39 |issue=5 |pages=747–753 |doi=10.1017/S0021963098002595|doi-broken-date=May 5, 2026 |pmid=9690937 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==== Executive function ====

[[Executive functions|Executive function]] is a high-order cognitive ability, which allows you to sustain, switch and inhibit cognitive functions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pribram |first1=Karl H. |last2=McGuinness |first2=Diane |date=1975 |title=Arousal, activation, and effort in the control of attention. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/h0076780 |journal=Psychological Review |language=en |volume=82 |issue=2 |pages=116–149 |doi=10.1037/h0076780 |pmid=1096213 |issn=1939-1471|url-access=subscription }}</ref> It is involved in the processes of coordination, planning and goal-directed actions.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Executive function: development across the life span |date=2018 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-315-16071-9 |editor-last=Karbach |editor-first=Julia |edition=First |series=Frontiers of developmental science |location=New York |editor-last2=Wiebe |editor-first2=Sandra A.}}</ref> Executive function emerges in the first year of life, and strengthens throughout childhood and adolescence.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Best |first1=John R. |last2=Miller |first2=Patricia H. |date=2010 |title=A Developmental Perspective on Executive Function |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=81 |issue=6 |pages=1641–1660 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8624.2010.01499.x |issn=0009-3920 |pmc=3058827 |pmid=21077853}}</ref> Cognitive abilities in infancy are predictive of executive functions at age 11, having long-term implications on memory and attention.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rose |first1=Susan A. |last2=Feldman |first2=Judith F. |last3=Jankowski |first3=Jeffery J. |date=2012 |title=Implications of Infant Cognition for Executive Functions at Age 11 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0956797612444902 |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=23 |issue=11 |pages=1345–1355 |doi=10.1177/0956797612444902 |pmid=23027882 |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Cultural differences in development ==
Most of the key stages of infant cognitive development appear universal across many cultures, emerging reliability and at similar times.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Kärtner |first1=Joscha |title=Chapter 11 - Culture and early social-cognitive development |date=2020-01-01 |journal=Progress in Brain Research |volume=254 |pages=225–246 |editor-last=Hunnius |editor-first=Sabine |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0079612320300868 |access-date=2025-04-10 |series=New Perspectives on Early Social-cognitive Development |publisher=Elsevier |doi=10.1016/bs.pbr.2020.06.011 |last2=Schuhmacher |first2=Nils |last3=Giner Torréns |first3=Marta |pmid=32859289 |isbn=978-0-12-820516-7 |editor2-last=Meyer |editor2-first=Marlene|url-access=subscription }}</ref> However there appears to be a lack of uniformity across how and when these abilities develop, including differences in cognitive styles and pathways of development which are dependent on cultural beliefs and practices.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Dasen |first=Pierre R. |date=2022-08-01 |title=Culture and Cognitive Development |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00220221221092409 |journal=Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology |language=EN |volume=53 |issue=7–8 |pages=789–816 |doi=10.1177/00220221221092409 |issn=0022-0221|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Executive function ===

East Asian children outperform western children in executive function, with bilingual children showing better executive function abilities than monolingual children.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schirmbeck |first1=Katharina |last2=Rao |first2=Nirmala |last3=Maehler |first3=Claudia |date=2020 |title=Similarities and differences across countries in the development of executive functions in children: A systematic review |journal=Infant and Child Development |language=en |volume=29 |issue=1 |article-number=e2164 |doi=10.1002/icd.2164 |issn=1522-7219|doi-access=free }}</ref> The executive function advantage of eastern cultures can be contributed to a number of factors:<ref>{{Cite web |last1=Roos |first1=Leslie |last2=Beauchamp |first2=Kathryn |last3=Flannery |first3=Jessica |last4=Fisher |first4=Philip |date=2017 |title=Cultural Contributions to Childhood Executive Function |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/319777098 |access-date=4 April 2025 |website=Research Gate}}</ref>

'''Value systems''' - Eastern education systems can promote higher processing speeds. The routines and hobbies of eastern cultures may also improve executive function, requiring speed and flexibility.

'''Parenting norms''' - Emotional socialization in eastern parenting promotes earlier and better executive function.

'''Language''' - bilingualism and high language proficiency increases executive function.

=== Memory ===
While basic memory development and processes are universal, the content, organization and expression of memory is influenced by culture. For example, verbal scaffolding and linear memory styles in the west promote improved autobiographical memory in western children. In the east, repetitive and directive memory styles promote recall of social events, making memory thematic and holistic.<ref>{{Citation |last=Kolling |first=Thorsten |title=Memory Development from Infancy to Childhood: Cross-cultural Perspectives |date=2022-03-29 |work=The Development of Memory in Infancy and Childhood |pages=286–296 |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/9781003016533/chapters/10.4324/9781003016533-12 |access-date=2025-04-10 |edition=3 |place=London |publisher=Psychology Press |language=en |doi=10.4324/9781003016533-12 |isbn=978-1-003-01653-3|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Language ===
Parents in the west practice more infant-directed speech, encouraging early verbal interaction and faster language development. Eastern children who experience less direct verbal interaction and more observation of speech than practice may be slower to develop speech.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Kuchirko |first1=Yana A. |title=Chapter Two - The cultural context of infant development: Variability, specificity, and universality |date=2019-01-01 |journal=Advances in Child Development and Behavior |volume=57 |pages=27–63 |editor-last=Henry |editor-first=Daphne A. |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0065240719300175 |access-date=2025-04-10 |series=Child Development at the Intersection of Race and SES |publisher=JAI |doi=10.1016/bs.acdb.2019.04.004 |last2=Tamis-LeMonda |first2=Catherine S. |pmid=31296318 |editor2-last=Votruba-Drzal |editor2-first=Elizabeth |editor3-last=Miller |editor3-first=Portia|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Internationally adopted children experience delays in speech development due to the abrupt shift in linguistic and cultural environment, becoming exposed to a new language in the process of second first language acquisition. While most catch-up by age 2, they appear weaker in vocabulary depth and complex grammar<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Glennen |first=Sharon |date=2002 |title=Language Development and Delay in Internationally Adoped Infants and Toddlers: A Review |url=http://pubs.asha.org/doi/10.1044/1058-0360%282002/038%29 |journal=American Journal of Speech-Language Pathology |language=en |volume=11 |issue=4 |pages=333–339 |doi=10.1044/1058-0360(2002/038) |issn=1058-0360|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
